title: The 318 Holy Fathers of Nicaea — St. Simon the Zealot & St. Hesychius the Palatine
narrative:
The 318 Holy and God-bearing Fathers of the First Ecumenical Council of Nicaea, St. Simon the Zealot the Apostle of the Seventy, and St. Hesychius the Palatine Soldier-Martyr

On the tenth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates one of the great conciliar feasts of the Ethiopian year: the commemoration of the three hundred and eighteen Holy Fathers who gathered at Nicaea in the year three hundred and twenty-five at the summons of the Emperor Constantine to resolve the crisis of the Arian heresy and to define, once and for all, the faith of the Church concerning the divine nature of the Son. The number three hundred and eighteen is not incidental: the tradition reads it as the number of Abraham's servants in Genesis fourteen, the armed men who pursued the kings and rescued Lot, and interprets it as a sign that the Fathers were not merely ecclesiastical administrators but soldiers of the Word, armed with the faith of the apostles and sent to rescue the captive truth from the distortions that had imprisoned it. Nicaea is the Church's greatest act of theological self-definition, and its Fathers are commemorated not as individuals but as a corporate body, a council, a single voice through whose many mouths the one Holy Spirit spoke the one definitive word.

The theology of Nicaea, in the Ethiopian reading, is inseparable from the theology of baptism. The Nicene Creed — which the three hundred and eighteen Fathers produced, and which the Council of Constantinople later completed — is the baptismal confession of the universal Church, the statement of faith into which every Christian is baptised and to which every Christian returns in every liturgy. The Ethiopian church chants the Creed at every Eucharist not merely as a doctrinal recitation but as a liturgical act of re-entering the baptismal waters, of dying and rising again with Christ in the words through which the Church has defined what that dying and rising means. To commemorate the Fathers of Nicaea is to commemorate the gift of clarity: the moment when the Church, dispersed across the empire and speaking many languages, gathered in one place and found that it could say one thing together.

Saint Simon the Zealot — so named either for his membership in the Jewish nationalist movement before his call or for the quality of his subsequent apostolic fervour — is one of the least documented of the Twelve in the canonical Gospels, yet the tradition of his missionary activity is among the most geographically extensive of any apostle, carrying him according to various accounts into Persia, Egypt, and some Ethiopian traditions place him in Abyssinia itself. His presence on the feast of the Nicene Fathers is the tradition's statement about the relationship between the original apostolic proclamation and its subsequent conciliar definition: Nicaea did not create the faith that Simon preached; it defended and articulated what the apostles had handed on. The Zealot who preached among the Persians and the Fathers who defined the homoousios are transmitting the same deposit, in different forms and centuries.

Saint Hesychius the Palatine is the great discovery of this day: a soldier of the imperial palace guard who was among a group of soldiers identified as Christian during a purification ritual of the military, in which soldiers were ordered to offer incense to the imperial gods or face dismissal. Hesychius, when he heard the order, removed his military belt — the distinctive mark of Roman military service — laid it before his commanding officer, and declared himself willing to be dismissed rather than offer the incense. He was arrested, tortured, and drowned in a river with a stone tied to his neck. His gesture of removing the belt before the command could be enforced — the voluntary relinquishment of military dignity before the compulsory stripping that would follow — is one of the tradition's most eloquent images of the free martyr: not one who waits to be stripped but one who strips himself, not one who loses honour but one who redefines it. The soldier who laid down his belt before Nicaea, and the three hundred and eighteen who took up the spiritual arms of faith at Nicaea, are witnesses to the same truth from opposite ends of the same history. Amen.
